active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi for geometric radius computation
active_paradigms.description: Transform the problem by computing radii geometrically from Voronoi diagram instead of optimizing them. Design initial positions with corner-heavy asymmetric pattern (4 large corners, 12 edges, 10 interior in hexagonal arrangement). Compute scipy.spatial.Voronoi diagram for these positions. For each circle, set radius to the minimum of: half the distance to each Voronoi neighbor (where circles would touch) and distance to the square boundary (x, 1-x, y, 1-y). This guarantees valid packing by construction - no overlaps possible. The positions can be designed to maximize the resulting Voronoi-computed radii, exploiting the geometric relationship between position spacing and achievable radius.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Circle positions designed to maximize Voronoi-computed radii; radii are computed deterministically from geometry
active_paradigms.cautions: Handle Voronoi cells at square boundaries correctly - finite Voronoi computation needs boundary reflection or clipping; ensure radius computation accounts for both Voronoi neighbors and square edges; the quality depends on position design

active_paradigms.description: Replace constraint-based optimization with penalty-based optimization using L-BFGS-B, which is faster and may find different local optima. Formulate objective as -sum(radii) + penalty_coeff * (overlap_penalty + boundary_penalty), where overlap_penalty = sum of max(0, r_i + r_j - distance_ij)^2 for all pairs and boundary_penalty = sum of max(0, x-r, r-1+x, y-r, r-1+y)^2. L-BFGS-B handles 78 variables efficiently and the penalty formulation avoids sqrt numerical issues. Use increasing penalty coefficients: start with penalty=100, run optimization, then increase penalty and restart from the solution to enforce constraints more strictly.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: All 78 variables (positions and radii) with penalty-weighted objective function
active_paradigms.cautions: Penalty coefficient must be large enough to make constraint violations costly; final solution must have near-zero penalty to be valid; use multiple random initializations like current approach; L-BFGS-B only handles box constraints, so other constraints must be penalties

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.fmin_cobyla with linear constraint approximation
active_paradigms.description: Apply COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) which handles constraints differently from SLSQP. COBYLA builds linear models of constraints in a trust region, while SLSQP uses quadratic approximations - this different strategy may find different local optima. Formulate constraints as functions returning positive values for feasible points: for each pair, constraint = distance - r_i - r_j; for boundaries, constraint = x - r, constraint = 1 - x - r, etc. Use corner-heavy initial placement. COBYLA is particularly suited for problems with many constraints (325+ here) and may navigate the constraint landscape more efficiently than SLSQP's quadratic approach.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: All 78 variables with explicit constraint functions evaluated at each iteration
active_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA constraint functions must return positive for feasible, negative for infeasible; use rhobeg parameter (initial step size) around 0.05-0.1; may need more iterations than SLSQP (set maxfun high); use multiple starting points as COBYLA is still a local method

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.differential_evolution with adaptive penalty constraints
tried_paradigms.description: Apply differential_evolution for global optimization with penalty-based constraint handling. Unlike basinhopping or dual_annealing (which failed), DE maintains a population and uses mutation/crossover operators specifically designed for continuous optimization. Encode solution as flat array [x0,y0,r0,x1,y1,r1,...]. Define penalty function: large penalty (1e6) for each constraint violation, scaled by violation amount. Use 'best1bin' strategy with population size 15-20. Set recombination parameter ~0.7, mutation ~0.5. Run for 1000+ generations with tolerance 1e-7.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: maximize sum of radii with constraint penalties
tried_paradigms.cautions: Penalty weights must be large enough to enforce constraints but not so large they create numerical issues. Use adaptive penalty: increase weight over generations. Bounds must be set correctly: centers in [0.05,0.95], radii in [0.01,0.15]. Polish with local optimizer at end (set polish=True). May take longer but can escape local optima that trap SLSQP.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Delaunay for contact graph construction with constraint solving
tried_paradigms.description: Build a contact-based packing using Delaunay triangulation: (1) Initialize 26 circles in a corner-optimized pattern with 4 large corners, ~12 edge circles, ~10 interior circles. (2) Compute scipy.spatial.Delaunay triangulation to identify neighbor relationships. (3) Set up a system of equations where each Delaunay edge represents a contact constraint: dist(i,j) = r_i + r_j. (4) Use scipy.optimize.fsolve or scipy.optimize.root to solve for positions and radii simultaneously. (5) For boundary circles, add edge-touching constraints. This approach directly enforces contacts rather than using Voronoi territories.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii by solving contact constraints as a system of equations rather than iterative territory optimization
tried_paradigms.cautions: Delaunay edges may not all correspond to actual contacts in optimal packing; need to identify which edges are 'active' contacts. Use a subset of edges based on distance threshold. Handle numerical precision carefully when solving the nonlinear system.

tried_paradigms.idea: Explicit asymmetric construction with corner-heavy radius distribution
tried_paradigms.description: Directly construct a packing optimized for unequal radii: (1) Place 4 corner circles at positions (r_c, r_c), (1-r_c, r_c), (r_c, 1-r_c), (1-r_c, 1-r_c) with radius r_c ≈ 0.12. (2) Place 3 edge circles along each side between corners at regular intervals with radius r_e ≈ 0.09. (3) Fill interior with hexagonal pattern of 10 circles with radius r_i ≈ 0.06. (4) Calculate exact radii from geometric constraints: for each circle, radius = min(distance to edges, distance to neighbors - neighbor_radius). (5) Use iterative radius assignment in priority order (corners first, then edges, then interior) to get a valid packing in one pass.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Sum of radii by exploiting the geometric advantage of corner positions - corner circles can be larger because they only need to avoid 2 edges and fewer neighbors
tried_paradigms.cautions: The greedy radius assignment order matters significantly - assigning corner radii first may limit interior circles too much. Consider using a more balanced approach where radii are computed from the constraint system rather than assigned greedily.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for constrained refinement
tried_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with trust-constr for the final optimization phase: (1) Use the current Voronoi+Lloyd initialization to get good starting positions. (2) Formulate the problem with 78 variables (x, y, r for each of 26 circles). (3) Define constraints using scipy.optimize.NonlinearConstraint for circle-circle non-overlap and boundary constraints. (4) Use trust-constr method which employs a trust-region approach that handles non-linear constraints more robustly than SLSQP, especially for problems with many constraints. (5) trust-constr uses interior-point methods and can better handle the coupling between position and radius variables.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary sum of radii by using a more robust constrained optimization method that doesn't get stuck in SLSQP's local minima
tried_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires defining constraints as a separate NonlinearConstraint object with Jacobian. Need to provide Jacobian of constraints for efficiency. The method may be slower than SLSQP but can find better solutions for highly constrained problems.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.basinhopping with SLSQP local minimizer for global optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Basinhopping is a stochastic global optimization algorithm that combines random perturbation with local minimization. Unlike pure SLSQP which gets trapped in local minima, basinhopping can escape by accepting worse solutions with some probability (Metropolis criterion). Use the current SLSQP formulation as the local minimizer within basinhopping. Set niter=100-200 for thorough exploration, temperature parameter to control acceptance of worse solutions, and use the existing constraint formulation. The key is that basinhopping explores the solution landscape globally while SLSQP finds local optima efficiently.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii by escaping local minima that trap pure SLSQP
tried_paradigms.cautions: Set appropriate temperature parameter (start with 0.5-1.0), use take_step callback to ensure perturbations stay feasible, limit niter to stay within timeout

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.shgo (simplicial homology global optimization) for rigorous global search
tried_paradigms.description: SHGO uses simplicial homology to partition the search space and find all local minima, then returns the global minimum. Unlike basinhopping's stochastic approach, SHGO is deterministic and provides theoretical guarantees on finding global optima for Lipschitz continuous functions. Use the existing SLSQP constraints but pass them to SHGO which handles both inequality constraints and bounds natively. Set sampling method='sobol' for better coverage of the high-dimensional space (78 variables for 26 circles). SHGO internally uses local minimization at sampled points.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: global optimum of sum of radii, avoiding all local minima traps
tried_paradigms.cautions: SHGO can be computationally expensive; set maxiter and maxtime appropriately, use iters=3-5 for moderate exploration, the constraint format differs slightly from minimize()

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.distance.pdist for vectorized contact constraint computation with iterative radius expansion
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of optimizing all variables simultaneously, use an iterative expansion approach. Start with small equal radii circles in good positions. At each iteration: compute all pairwise distances using pdist (vectorized, fast), identify the most constrained circle (smallest gap to neighbors/walls), expand that circle's radius until it contacts something, then recompute distances. This mimics physical bubble growth and naturally finds good contact topologies. Use scipy.spatial.Delaunay to identify neighbor relationships for efficient gap computation. The iterative nature ensures each circle reaches its maximum possible radius given the constraints.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: each circle's radius independently in sequence, building a dense contact network
tried_paradigms.cautions: Need to handle the order of expansion carefully (larger circles first often works better), must recompute distances after each expansion, boundary constraints need explicit checking

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with COBYLA method for constraint-based optimization
tried_paradigms.description: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) handles non-linear constraints differently than SLSQP - it builds linear approximations of constraints and objective. This can sometimes find better solutions in highly non-convex landscapes. Use the same corner-heavy initialization but with COBYLA's derivative-free approach which may navigate the constraint landscape differently. Set maxiter to 3000 and use rhobeg=0.05 for initial step size.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii by potentially finding different local optima
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA doesn't support bounds directly, so bounds must be encoded as inequality constraints. Use larger maxiter since COBYLA may need more iterations. The method is derivative-free which can be advantageous for non-smooth regions.

tried_paradigms.idea: Direct hexagonal lattice construction with boundary adaptation
tried_paradigms.description: Construct packing directly from geometric principles: (1) Place 4 corner circles with radius computed to maximize space (2) Fill edges with circles arranged in hexagonal contact pattern along each side (3) Fill interior with hexagonal lattice packing, computing exact positions using sqrt(3)/2 vertical spacing (4) Compute radii to maximize contacts - each interior circle touches 6 neighbors. Use analytical formulas for positions rather than optimization, then do a single refinement pass.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: packing density through optimal geometric arrangement
tried_paradigms.cautions: Hexagonal packing in a square requires careful boundary handling. The lattice spacing must be computed to fit 26 circles optimally. May need to adjust lattice orientation or offset to better fit the square container.

tried_paradigms.idea: Sequential greedy placement with maximum radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Build packing incrementally: (1) Place 4 corner circles at maximum possible radius (r = 1/(2+sqrt(2)) ≈ 0.293 if all equal, but optimize individually) (2) For each subsequent circle, compute the maximum radius it can have given all previously placed circles (3) Place each new circle at the position that maximizes its radius (4) Use scipy.optimize.minimize for each single-circle placement subproblem. This greedy approach avoids the complexity of optimizing all 78 variables simultaneously.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: each circle's radius independently while respecting constraints
tried_paradigms.cautions: Greedy placement order matters significantly - place larger circles first. The subproblems are simpler (3 variables each) but the global optimum may differ from greedy solution. Try different placement orders (corners→edges→interior vs other orderings).
